content.problemStatement:
Suppose for a unit rise in temperature, the solubility of sugar in water increases by Bg100 mLB \frac{\mathrm{g}}{100\ \mathrm{mL}}B100 mLg​.

Chef does an experiment to check how much sugar (in g\mathrm{g}g) he can dissolve given that he initially has 111 liter of water at XXX degrees and the solubility of sugar at this temperature is Ag100 mLA \frac{\mathrm{g}}{100\ \mathrm{mL}}A100 mLg​. Also, Chef doesn't want to lose any water so he can increase the temperature to at most 100100100 degrees.

Assuming no loss of water takes place during the process, find the maximum amount of sugar (in g\mathrm{g}g) can be dissolved in 111 liter of water under the given conditions.

###Input


The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The only line of each test case contains three integers X,A,BX, A, BX,A,B.
###Output
For each testcase, output in a single line the answer to the problem.

###Constraints


1≤T≤10001 \leq T \leq 10001≤T≤1000
31≤X≤4031 \leq X \leq 4031≤X≤40
101≤A≤120101 \leq A \leq 120101≤A≤120
1≤B≤51 \leq B \leq 51≤B≤5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers X, A and B:
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum amount of sugar (in grams) that can be dissolved in 1 litre of water under the given conditions.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
For each test case we raise the temperature to 100°C.
Test case 1: solubility = 120 + (100-40)*1 = 180 g/100mL, amount = 180*10 = 1800 g.
Test case 2: solubility = 120 + (100-35)*2 = 250 g/100mL, amount = 250*10 = 2500 g.
Test case 3: solubility = 115 + (100-40)*3 = 295 g/100mL, amount = 295*10 = 2950 g.

solution.approach: For a test case we can raise the temperature from the initial X up to the allowed maximum of 100 degrees. The temperature increase is (100 - X) degrees. Each degree adds B grams of sugar per 100 mL, so the final solubility is S = A + (100 - X) * B (grams per 100 mL). One litre equals 1000 mL = 10 × 100 mL, therefore the maximum amount of sugar that can be dissolved is answer = S * 10. The computation is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
